---
title: "Sixteen Years of 311: How Resolution Odds Changed Across Administrations"
subtitle: >
  Recorded resolution-time probabilities for NYC 311 service requests, 2010–2026,
  compared across mayoral administrations — citywide, by complaint type, and within
  individual census tracts.
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: darkly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-dpi: 150
execute:
  warning: false
  echo: true
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-note title="How to read this document"}
Plain-language takeaways open each section; the code that produced every number and
figure is folded beneath it (click "Code"). To reproduce: run the pipeline in
`pipeline/` (`fetch_311.py`, `fetch_311_history.py`, `prepare.py`, `prepare_hist.py`),
then render this file with `quarto render docs/historical_analysis.qmd`.
:::

## Summary

This analysis asks how the *recorded* probability that a 311 request gets resolved —
within 3 hours, within a day, within a week — has moved since 2010, and whether
different mayoral administrations are associated with different odds **in the same
census tract**. Three design choices matter throughout:

1. **Within-type comparisons.** What New Yorkers report changed enormously (the 311
   app made noise complaints explode), and complaint types resolve at wildly different
   speeds. Comparing administrations on the all-complaints average mostly measures
   this composition shift, so every comparison here is within a complaint type.
2. **Recorded, not necessarily real.** A "resolution" is the city closing the ticket.
   Closure practices (batch purges, auto-close policies) changed across eras; we flag
   and quantify these artifacts rather than pretend they don't exist.
3. **COVID is its own era.** March 2020 – December 2021 is a structural break and is
   never averaged into an administration's record.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| code-summary: "Setup: load 16 years of cleaned requests, define eras"
import glob, json, os, sys
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

ROOT = ".." if os.path.basename(os.getcwd()) == "docs" else "."
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join(ROOT, "pipeline"))
import model as M

ERAS = [
    ("bloomberg", "Bloomberg (final term)", "2010-01-01", "2014-01-01"),
    ("deblasio",  "de Blasio (pre-COVID)",  "2014-01-01", "2020-03-01"),
    ("covid",     "COVID emergency",        "2020-03-01", "2022-01-01"),
    ("adams",     "Adams",                  "2022-01-01", "2026-01-01"),
    ("current",   "Mamdani (2026)",         "2026-01-01", "2027-01-01"),
]
ERA_LABEL = {k: lb for k, lb, *_ in ERAS}

# conservative 1:1 taxonomy renames across the study period
RENAMES = {"HEATING": "HEAT/HOT WATER", "PAINT - PLASTER": "PAINT/PLASTER",
           "NONCONST": "UNSANITARY CONDITION", "Sewer Maintenance": "Sewer"}

parts = sorted(glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "data", "prepared_hist", "year=*.parquet")))
df = pd.concat([pd.read_parquet(p) for p in parts]
               + [pd.read_parquet(os.path.join(ROOT, "data", "prepared.parquet"))],
               ignore_index=True)
df["created_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["created_date"])
for c in ["geoid", "nta", "boro", "complaint_type"]:
    df[c] = df[c].astype(str)
df.loc[df["geoid"].isin(["nan", "None", "<NA>"]), "geoid"] = np.nan
df["complaint_type"] = df["complaint_type"].replace(RENAMES)
df["era"] = pd.cut(df["created_date"],
                   bins=[pd.Timestamp(s) for _, _, s, _ in ERAS] + [pd.Timestamp(ERAS[-1][3])],
                   labels=[k for k, *_ in ERAS], right=False)
df = df[df["era"].notna()]
df["year"] = df["created_date"].dt.year
N_TOTAL = len(df)
SPAN = (df["created_date"].min().date(), df["created_date"].max().date())
era_keys = [k for k, *_ in ERAS if (df["era"] == k).any()]   # eras with data present

# dark theme to match the map app
C = {"bg": "#0B0E14", "text": "#F2F4F8", "sub": "#9AA3B5", "line": "#2A3244",
     "accent": "#FFB84D"}
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor": C["bg"], "axes.facecolor": C["bg"], "savefig.facecolor": C["bg"],
    "axes.edgecolor": C["line"], "axes.labelcolor": C["sub"], "text.color": C["text"],
    "xtick.color": C["sub"], "ytick.color": C["sub"], "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.color": C["line"], "grid.linewidth": 0.5, "font.size": 11})
```

The analysis covers **`{python} f"{N_TOTAL:,}"`** cleaned, matured service requests
created between `{python} str(SPAN[0])` and `{python} str(SPAN[1])`, all passed
through the identical hygiene rules as the live map (de-duplication, double-submission
collapse, negative/zero-duration removal, batch-closure flagging, point-in-polygon
assignment to 2020 census tracts — see `docs/METHODS.md`).

## The data changed as much as the city did

Before comparing administrations, look at what the instrument itself was doing.
Request volume roughly doubled; the complaint mix transformed; geocoding quality
improved; and the share of requests never administratively closed varies by era.
Every one of these can masquerade as a "service change."

```{python}
#| label: artifacts
#| code-summary: "Era context table: volume, composition, artifact rates"
rows = []
for key, label, s, e in ERAS:
    sub = df[df["era"] == key]
    if len(sub) == 0:
        continue
    yrs = max((pd.Timestamp(e) - pd.Timestamp(s)).days / 365.25, 5 / 12)
    top3 = sub["complaint_type"].value_counts(normalize=True).head(3)
    rows.append({
        "era": label,
        "requests/yr (M)": round(len(sub) / yrs / 1e6, 2),
        "share never closed": f'{(sub["bin"].eq(M.K - 1) & 1).mean():.1%}',
        "batch-closure flagged": f'{sub["batch_closed"].mean():.1%}',
        "tract-geocoded": f'{sub["geoid"].notna().mean():.1%}',
        "top complaint (share)": f"{top3.index[0].title()} ({top3.iloc[0]:.0%})",
    })
pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("era")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="What this table means for interpretation"}
The comparisons below are of **recorded closures under each era's record-keeping
practices**. Where an era's batch-closure or never-closed rates stand out, treat its
differences as partly administrative. Tract geocoding also improves over time; rows
without coordinates still inform borough and citywide estimates but not tract-level
ones.
:::

```{python}
#| label: stable-types
#| code-summary: "Select complaint types comparable across all five eras"
MIN_PER_ERA = 15_000
vol = df.groupby(["complaint_type", "era"], observed=True).size().unstack("era", fill_value=0)
stable = vol[(vol >= MIN_PER_ERA).all(axis=1)].sort_values("adams", ascending=False)
TYPES = stable.index.tolist()
```

Cross-era comparisons use the **`{python} len(TYPES)`** complaint types that appear
with meaningful volume (≥15,000 requests) in *every* era, after normalizing known
renames (e.g., pre-2014 "HEATING" is today's "Heat/Hot Water"):
`{python} ", ".join(t.title() for t in TYPES)`.

## The long view: same-day resolution, 2010–2026

```{python}
#| label: fig-annual
#| fig-cap: "Share of requests resolved within 24 hours, by year. Vertical lines mark administration changes; the dashed span is the COVID emergency period."
#| code-summary: "Annual P(resolved ≤ 24h) by complaint type"
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 6))
palette = ["#FFB84D", "#7AD151", "#2A788E", "#BDDF26", "#F5A97F", "#43D9A3",
           "#7AA7FF", "#D183C9"]
sel = ["ALL"] + TYPES[:6]
for i, t in enumerate(sel):
    sub = df if t == "ALL" else df[df["complaint_type"] == t]
    ann = sub.groupby("year")["bin"].apply(lambda s: (s <= 1).mean())
    ax.plot(ann.index, ann.values, color=palette[i % len(palette)],
            lw=2.6 if t == "ALL" else 1.4, alpha=1 if t == "ALL" else .9,
            label="All complaints" if t == "ALL" else t.title())
for x in [2014, 2022, 2026]:
    ax.axvline(x - .5, color=C["line"], lw=1)
ax.axvspan(2020.17, 2022 - .5, color="#FFFFFF", alpha=.045)
for x, name in [(2011.5, "Bloomberg"), (2017, "de Blasio"), (2020.9, "COVID"),
                (2023.7, "Adams"), (2026.1, "Mamdani")]:
    ax.text(x, 1.02, name, transform=ax.get_xaxis_transform(), ha="center",
            fontsize=9, color=C["sub"])
ax.set_ylim(0, 1); ax.set_xlim(2009.6, 2026.6)
ax.set_ylabel("share resolved within 24 hours")
ax.legend(loc="lower left", frameon=False, fontsize=9, ncols=2)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: mix-adjust
#| code-summary: "Composition-adjusted citywide same-day rate"
ctype_all = df["complaint_type"].where(df["complaint_type"].isin(TYPES), "Other")
mix = ctype_all.value_counts(normalize=True)                     # fixed all-era mix
era_type_p24 = (df.assign(ct=ctype_all).groupby(["era", "ct"], observed=True)["bin"]
                  .apply(lambda s: (s <= 1).mean()).unstack("ct"))
raw_all = df.groupby("era", observed=True)["bin"].apply(lambda s: (s <= 1).mean())
adj_all = (era_type_p24 * mix).sum(axis=1)
adj = pd.DataFrame({"raw P(≤24h)": raw_all.round(3), "mix-adjusted": adj_all.round(3)})
adj.index = [ERA_LABEL[k] for k in adj.index]
adj
```

The gap between the raw and mix-adjusted columns is the composition effect: how much
of each era's apparent speed comes from *what was reported* rather than *how fast it
was handled*. Interpretation in the prose below uses the adjusted view.

## Era × complaint type

```{python}
#| label: fig-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Recorded share of requests resolved within 24 hours, by era and complaint type."
#| code-summary: "Era × type heatmap"
tt = ["ALL"] + TYPES
mat = np.full((len(tt), len(era_keys)), np.nan)
for j, k in enumerate(era_keys):
    sub = df[df["era"] == k]
    mat[0, j] = (sub["bin"] <= 1).mean()
    for i, t in enumerate(TYPES, start=1):
        s2 = sub.loc[sub["complaint_type"] == t, "bin"]
        if len(s2):
            mat[i, j] = (s2 <= 1).mean()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, .45 * len(tt) + 2))
ax.imshow(mat, cmap="viridis", vmin=0, vmax=1, aspect="auto")
ax.set_xticks(range(len(era_keys)), [ERA_LABEL[k] for k in era_keys],
              rotation=18, ha="right", fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(tt)),
              ["All complaints" if t == "ALL" else t.title() for t in tt], fontsize=9)
for i in range(len(tt)):
    for j in range(len(era_keys)):
        if not np.isnan(mat[i, j]):
            ax.text(j, i, f"{mat[i,j]:.0%}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8,
                    color="#0B0E14" if mat[i, j] > .55 else "#F2F4F8")
ax.grid(False); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## Within the same tract: administration deltas

The citywide picture can hide local reversals. Here we fit the **same hierarchical
Bayesian model that powers the live map** (tract → neighborhood → borough → city,
per complaint type; concentrations by marginal-likelihood MLE) separately to each
era's data, then difference the posterior same-day probabilities tract by tract.
Only tracts with at least 30 requests of that type in *both* eras are compared, so
the deltas reflect local evidence rather than shared priors.

```{python}
#| label: era-fits
#| code-summary: "Fit the hierarchical model per era; compute tract-level deltas"
geo = M.GeoIndex(json.load(open(os.path.join(ROOT, "data", "geo_lookup.json"))))
MIN_TRACT_N = 30
tix = {t: i for i, t in enumerate(TYPES + ["Other"])}

fits, counts = {}, {}
for key in era_keys:
    sub = df[df["era"] == key].copy()
    sub["ctype"] = M.collapse_types(sub["complaint_type"], TYPES)
    fits[key] = M.fit(sub, geo, TYPES + ["Other"], M.Config("era", half_life_days=None),
                      sub["created_date"].max())
    s2 = sub[sub["geoid"].notna()]
    counts[key] = s2.groupby([s2["geoid"], s2["ctype"]], observed=True).size()

def p24_and_var(fm):
    a = fm.a_tract; A = a.sum(-1); Ac = a[..., :2].sum(-1)
    m = Ac / A
    return m, m * (1 - m) / (A + 1)

def tract_deltas(a_key, b_key):
    ma, va = p24_and_var(fits[a_key]); mb, vb = p24_and_var(fits[b_key])
    out = {}
    for t in TYPES:
        ti = tix[t]; recs = []
        for g, gi in geo.tract_ix.items():
            if counts[a_key].get((g, t), 0) < MIN_TRACT_N: continue
            if counts[b_key].get((g, t), 0) < MIN_TRACT_N: continue
            d = ma[gi, ti] - mb[gi, ti]
            se = np.sqrt(va[gi, ti] + vb[gi, ti])
            recs.append((g, d, se))
        if len(recs) >= 25:
            dd = np.array([r[1] for r in recs])
            out[t] = {"recs": recs, "n": len(recs),
                      "med": np.median(dd),
                      "q10": np.percentile(dd, 10), "q90": np.percentile(dd, 90),
                      "up": np.mean([d - 1.645 * s > 0 for _, d, s in recs]),
                      "dn": np.mean([d + 1.645 * s < 0 for _, d, s in recs])}
    return out

D_ad = tract_deltas("adams", "deblasio")
D_ca = tract_deltas("current", "adams")

def delta_table(D):
    return pd.DataFrame({
        "tracts compared": {t.title(): v["n"] for t, v in D.items()},
        "median Δ (pts)": {t.title(): round(100 * v["med"], 1) for t, v in D.items()},
        "10th–90th pct": {t.title(): f'{100*v["q10"]:+.0f} … {100*v["q90"]:+.0f}'
                          for t, v in D.items()},
        "share sig. faster": {t.title(): f'{v["up"]:.0%}' for t, v in D.items()},
        "share sig. slower": {t.title(): f'{v["dn"]:.0%}' for t, v in D.items()},
    }).sort_values("median Δ (pts)", ascending=False)

```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-adams-deblasio
#| tbl-cap: 'Adams vs de Blasio (pre-COVID): change in P(resolved ≤ 24h) within the same tract, by complaint type. "Sig." = the tract''s 90% interval on the change excludes zero.'
#| code-summary: "Delta table: Adams vs de Blasio"
delta_table(D_ad)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-delta-map
#| fig-cap: "Tract-level change in same-day resolution probability, Adams era minus de Blasio (pre-COVID) era, for the mapped complaint type. Green = faster under Adams; purple = slower. Gray tracts lack 30+ requests in both eras."
#| code-summary: "Choropleth of tract deltas"
from matplotlib.collections import PolyCollection
from matplotlib.colors import TwoSlopeNorm

t_map = max(D_ad, key=lambda t: D_ad[t]["n"])       # best-covered type
dm = {g: d for g, d, _ in D_ad[t_map]["recs"]}
gj = json.load(open(os.path.join(ROOT, "web", "data", "tracts.geojson")))
polys, vals = [], []
for f in gj["features"]:
    geom, g = f["geometry"], f["properties"]["geoid"]
    rings = [geom["coordinates"][0]] if geom["type"] == "Polygon" \
        else [p[0] for p in geom["coordinates"]]
    for r in rings:
        polys.append(r); vals.append(dm.get(g, np.nan))
vals = np.array(vals, dtype=float)
vmax = max(np.nanpercentile(np.abs(vals), 95), .02)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 8.5))
ax.add_collection(PolyCollection([p for p, v in zip(polys, vals) if np.isnan(v)],
                                 facecolor="#1A2030", edgecolor=C["line"], linewidth=.1))
pc = PolyCollection([p for p, v in zip(polys, vals) if not np.isnan(v)],
                    array=vals[~np.isnan(vals)], cmap="PRGn",
                    norm=TwoSlopeNorm(vcenter=0, vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax),
                    edgecolor=C["line"], linewidth=.1)
ax.add_collection(pc); ax.autoscale(); ax.set_aspect(1.3); ax.axis("off")
cb = fig.colorbar(pc, ax=ax, shrink=.6, pad=.01)
cb.set_label(f"Δ P(≤24h): {t_map.title()}", color=C["sub"])
cb.ax.yaxis.set_tick_params(color=C["sub"])
ax.set_title(f"{t_map.title()} — same-day resolution, Adams minus de Blasio",
             fontsize=12, color=C["text"])
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-current-adams
#| tbl-cap: "Mamdani vs Adams — provisional: only ~5 months of matured 2026 data."
#| code-summary: "Mamdani administration vs Adams (provisional)"
delta_table(D_ca) if D_ca else "Too little 2026 data yet for tract-level comparison."
```

## Interpretation

*(This section is written against the rendered numbers above; the inline values
update automatically on re-render.)*

```{python}
#| label: interp-values
#| code-summary: "Values referenced in the prose"
p24 = lambda k, t=None: (era_type_p24.loc[k, t] if t
                         else raw_all.loc[k])
noise_types = [t for t in TYPES if "Noise" in t]
noise_share = {k: df[df["era"] == k]["complaint_type"].isin(noise_types).mean()
               for k in era_keys}
raw_gain = raw_all.loc["adams"] - raw_all.loc["bloomberg"]
adj_gain = adj_all.loc["adams"] - adj_all.loc["bloomberg"]
covid_noise = noise_share.get("covid", float("nan"))
```

Findings that survive the composition and artifact controls:

1. **Most of the all-complaints "improvement" is composition.** Raw same-day
   resolution rose from `{python} f"{raw_all.loc['bloomberg']:.0%}"` (Bloomberg) to
   `{python} f"{raw_all.loc['adams']:.0%}"` (Adams) — but holding the complaint mix
   fixed, the rise is only `{python} f"{adj_all.loc['bloomberg']:.0%}"` →
   `{python} f"{adj_all.loc['adams']:.0%}"`. Roughly
   `{python} f"{1 - adj_gain/raw_gain:.0%}"` of the apparent improvement is the mix
   shifting toward fast-resolving categories (noise complaints grew from
   `{python} f"{noise_share['bloomberg']:.0%}"` of Bloomberg-era volume to
   `{python} f"{noise_share['adams']:.0%}"` under Adams). Any claim that "311 got
   faster under mayor X" that doesn't condition on complaint type is mostly measuring
   New Yorkers' reporting habits.
2. **The clearest operational pattern is agency-shaped, not administration-shaped.**
   Housing types (all handled by HPD) improved *monotonically across every era*:
   same-day resolution of Heat/Hot Water went
   `{python} f"{p24('bloomberg','HEAT/HOT WATER'):.0%}"` →
   `{python} f"{p24('deblasio','HEAT/HOT WATER'):.0%}"` →
   `{python} f"{p24('adams','HEAT/HOT WATER'):.0%}"` from Bloomberg to de Blasio to
   Adams, with Plumbing, Electric, and Paint/Plaster showing the same staircase from
   near-zero. Meanwhile infrastructure types slipped across the same span — Water
   System `{python} f"{p24('bloomberg','Water System'):.0%}"` →
   `{python} f"{p24('adams','Water System'):.0%}"`, Traffic Signals
   `{python} f"{p24('bloomberg','Traffic Signal Condition'):.0%}"` →
   `{python} f"{p24('adams','Traffic Signal Condition'):.0%}"` — and police-handled
   types (parking, noise) have been saturated near 99% for sixteen years. Secular
   agency trajectories dominate administration boundaries.
3. **Within types, administrations were not uniform across neighborhoods** — see
   @tbl-adams-deblasio: the 10th–90th percentile spread of tract-level changes is
   wide even where the median change is small, and the tract map shows spatial
   clustering rather than uniform shifts.
4. **COVID was a composition shock more than a slowdown.** The raw all-complaints
   series *rose* during 2020–21, because lockdown-era noise complaints surged to
   `{python} f"{covid_noise:.0%}"` of all volume; per-type effects ran in both
   directions (heat improved, derelict-vehicle and traffic-signal work slowed). This
   is exactly why the COVID window is quarantined and why the mix-adjusted row is
   the one to read.
5. **The Mamdani administration's record is provisional** (@tbl-current-adams).
   Zohran Mamdani took office in January 2026, succeeding Eric Adams, so this era is
   only five months of matured data, seasonally unbalanced (winter/spring only), and best
   treated as a baseline for future updates rather than a verdict — its lower heat
   number vs Adams, for instance, reflects an all-winter sample.

## Why the live map still trains on recent data only

The dashboard deliberately ignores this history when estimating *current* odds. Its
evaluation selected a 90-day exponential decay: under that weighting a two-year-old
request carries weight `{python} f"{2**(-730/90):.4f}"` — effectively zero — and the
prior comparison showed shorter memory beats longer memory for near-term prediction
(`docs/evaluation_results.md`). History answers "what changed"; the last two years
answer "what should I expect if I call today." Those are different questions, and
this document exists so the map doesn't have to answer both.

## Caveats, stated plainly

- **Recorded ≠ experienced.** Closure timestamps are administrative events. Era
  differences partly reflect record-keeping practice; batch-closure and never-closed
  rates are quantified in the era context table.
- **Bloomberg = final term only.** The public dataset begins 2010; nothing here
  speaks to 2002–2009.
- **Geocoding improves over time** (86% of 2010 requests are tract-mappable vs ~98%
  today), so early-era tract comparisons rest on a slightly thinner, possibly
  non-random subset of requests.
- **Taxonomy drift is handled conservatively**: only unambiguous renames are merged,
  and analysis is restricted to types present in every era. Types born after 2014
  (e.g., outdoor dining complaints) are simply out of scope for the long arc.
- **Administrations are periods, not causes.** Nothing here identifies *why* a
  probability moved — budgets, laws, weather, and reporting behavior all changed
  concurrently. This is a descriptive account of associations within fixed geography.

## Technical addendum: modeling decisions, sensitivity, and robustness {#sec-addendum}

This appendix records the decisions behind the machinery this analysis reuses, and the
checks run along the way. Numbers quoted from prior evaluations are the documented
outputs of the pipeline's own harnesses (`docs/evaluation_results.md`,
`docs/statistical_review.md`); everything specific to *this* document is computed in
the chunks above.

### Model class

The estimator throughout is a hierarchical Dirichlet–Multinomial over the nine ordered
duration bins, with partial pooling tract → NTA → borough → citywide, fit as a
top-down conjugate cascade (each node's Dirichlet prior is κ pseudo-counts shaped like
its parent's posterior mean). Alternatives considered and rejected: continuous-time
survival models (non-conjugate inference at 40M rows for a deliverable that is the
binned distribution anyway); continuation-ratio Beta–Binomial stacks (equally
conjugate but no actual adjacent-bin smoothing without breaking conjugacy — bin order
is instead respected at evaluation time via the ranked probability score); and full
hierarchical Bayes with sampled concentrations (intractable for monthly refresh; the
parent nodes are so data-rich that empirical-Bayes plug-in loses little). Pooling runs
within complaint type across geography, not across types within a tract, because
resolution speed is owned by the responsible agency, not the neighborhood.

### Configuration contest (live map)

Eight configurations were compared on a strict temporal holdout (train 2025-01 →
2026-01, test 2026-01 → 2026-06; all hyperparameters fit on train only), scored by
mean RPS (primary), log-loss, calibration error, and interval coverage, with paired
block-bootstrap SEs over tract×type cells:

| configuration | RPS | verdict |
|---|---|---|
| No pooling (uniform / Jeffreys) | 0.1096 / 0.1086 | collapse to log(9) log-loss on unseen cells |
| Hierarchy, fixed κ=15 | 0.1074 | pooling helps even hand-tuned |
| Hierarchy, EB κ (per type / per type-level) | 0.1070 / 0.1070 | learned pooling |
| **+ 90-day decay (shipped)** | **0.1064** | best; advantage concentrated in near-term months |
| + 180-day / 365-day decay | 0.1067 / 0.1068 | longer memory loses |

The no-pooling baselines' failure mode is exact and diagnostic: on tract×type cells
unseen in training they predict the bare prior — uniform over nine bins, log-loss
log(9) = 2.197 — while the hierarchy falls back to the neighborhood estimate (~1.44).

### Two defects found by audit, both fixed

An adversarial computational audit (independent re-implementation of the cascade;
verification of all 51,150 exported cells) found:

1. **Concentration estimation under-converged.** Minka's fixed-point iteration stalled
   far short of the marginal-likelihood optimum on these flat surfaces (tract-level
   Heat/Hot Water: κ ≈ 230 vs true optimum ≈ 800, a 78-nat likelihood gap;
   Illegal Parking: 87 vs ≈ 400, 288 nats). The estimator now maximizes the exact
   Dirichlet–Multinomial marginal likelihood by bounded scalar optimization.
   Predictions moved negligibly (the flat surface is why the defect was silent), but
   pooling strengths are now genuine MLEs — including the era fits above.
2. **Raw posterior intervals badly under-covered.** At nominal 90%, dense-cell
   coverage of realized near-future rates was 0.25–0.51 — including on an even/odd
   day-of-year split where drift is impossible by construction, refuting an initial
   "temporal drift" explanation. Real request outcomes are correlated within cells
   (repeat reports, batch closures, day-of-week structure) and the service regime
   itself moves. The live map's intervals now add a per-type, per-threshold regime
   variance estimated from rolling 60-day temporal holdouts: backtest coverage
   0.90–0.92, fully out-of-sample check 0.87. A multiplicative (design-effect)
   correction was tested and rejected — it absurdly inflates sparse-cell intervals;
   the additive form leaves them sampling-dominated.

### Sensitivity and robustness checks run

- **Decay half-life**: 90/180/365-day variants scored month-by-month; the 90-day
  advantage decays over the forecast horizon exactly as a drift-tracking
  interpretation predicts (and motivates the monthly refresh cycle).
- **Seasonal blending**: a same-season-last-year kernel (conjugate, weight
  β·2^(−|age−1yr|/bw)) was evaluated under a rolling-monthly protocol mirroring
  deployment. Best variant: ΔRPS −0.00001 ± 0.00008 vs recency-only — a null — with
  heavier blends slightly worse. Not adopted; scheduled for re-test once ≥2 full years
  of history exist (`pipeline/eval_seasonal.py`). This analysis's 16-year archive now
  makes that re-test possible.
- **Censoring**: the 31-day maturity cutoff is keyed to the top bin boundary, making
  the "still open ⇒ month+" assignment deductively exact rather than imputed;
  verified against pull dates. Composition of the month+ bin was audited: 8.8% of
  matured requests land there, 28.5% of those never close; for Heat/Hot Water >99% of
  month+ cases are administrative non-closures — reported as-is with a
  high-open-share flag.
- **Cleaning funnel** (live window): 5.34M → 5.04M rows; ~188k double-submissions
  collapsed, ~97k exact-zero durations dropped, ~85k batch closures flagged and
  *kept* (dropping them would optimistically bias slow agencies). A full evaluation
  re-run excluding batch-flagged records remains open; for this document their
  per-era rates are disclosed in the era context table instead.
- **RPS implementation** verified against hand computation; bootstrap resamples
  cells, not requests (within-cell correlation would otherwise understate SEs).

### Choices specific to this historical analysis

- **Era fits use no decay**: the estimand is each era's average recorded behavior,
  not a nowcast. Concentrations are re-estimated per era, so pooling strength adapts
  to each era's data density.
- **Stable-type restriction** (≥15k requests in every era) plus conservative 1:1
  rename normalization; types that split or merged across the 2014 taxonomy overhaul
  are excluded rather than guessed at.
- **Tract deltas require n ≥ 30 in both eras** so differences reflect local evidence
  rather than two shared priors agreeing with each other; interval on the delta is
  the normal combination of the two independent Beta variances. This intentionally
  reports *sampling* uncertainty for a fixed-era estimand — the regime-variance
  component the live map adds is a forecasting correction and would be wrong here.
- **Composition adjustment** fixes the complaint mix at the pooled all-era
  distribution; the raw-vs-adjusted gap isolates how much of each era's apparent
  speed is reporting mix.
- **COVID quarantine** (2020-03 – 2021-12) keeps an extraordinary operating regime
  out of both adjacent administrations' records.
- **Known residual risks**: geocoding coverage drift (86% → 98% tract-mappable);
  recorded-vs-experienced resolution; single-observation seasonality in the
  five-month Mamdani era (2026).

### Reproduction

```
python3 pipeline/fetch_311.py            # live window (2025-)
python3 pipeline/fetch_311_history.py    # 2010-2024 archive (~38M rows)
python3 pipeline/prepare.py && python3 pipeline/prepare_hist.py --all
quarto render docs/historical_analysis.qmd
```

Full machine-generated detail: `docs/model_spec.md` (specification),
`docs/evaluation_results.md` (configuration contest), `docs/statistical_review.md`
(audit), `docs/METHODS.md` (public methods).

---

*Data: NYC Open Data — 311 Service Requests (2010–2019 archive `76ig-c548`; 2020–present
`erm2-nwe9`). Cleaning and model code: `pipeline/` in this repository. Not affiliated
with the City of New York.*
